coredata.dc:description: A dual-metal orbital engineering strategy is developed to synergistically optimize active site reactivity and bulk-phase stability through precise d-band center modulation and enhanced charge transfer kinetics. Specifically, a flower-like NiMn selenide nanoarchitecture, modified with silver nanoparticles and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs), is fabricated via controlled spin state regulation. The asymmetric configuration between NiSe2 and MnSe induces a significant elevation in the energy levels of Ni’s eg and t2g orbitals, thereby optimizing intermediate conductivity, eg orbital electron occupancy, and adsorption free energy. Simultaneously, the reduced energy levels of Mn’s eg and t2g orbitals strengthen the interaction between Ni 3d/Mn 3d and Se 2p orbitals, promoting synergistic coupling, optimizing the electronic structure, and ensuring electrocatalytic stability. Consequently, the optimized catalyst exhibits remarkable performance, requiring overpotentials of 54.0 ± 0.5 mV and 235.0 ± 0.5 mV for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER), respectively, at 10 mA cm−2, while maintaining excellent stability. This work establishes a universal paradigm for d-band center manipulation in electrocatalyst design, providing critical insights for developing advanced energy conversion systems.

head.ce:title: Spin-state-regulated dual-metal orbital engineering in heterostructured nanosheets for d-band center-optimized electrocatalytic water splitting

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this paper and should be considered as co-first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-metal strategy creates a multiphase Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs nanoarchitecture, enhancing active site reactivity and stability via d-band center modulation and improved charge transfer. This advances electrocatalyst design for energy conversion systems.

A dual-metal orbital engineering strategy is developed to synergistically optimize active site reactivity and bulk-phase stability through precise d-band center modulation and enhanced charge transfer kinetics. Specifically, a flower-like NiMn selenide nanoarchitecture, modified with silver nanoparticles and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs), is fabricated via controlled spin state regulation. The asymmetric configuration between NiSe2 and MnSe induces a significant elevation in the energy levels of Ni’s eg and t2g orbitals, thereby optimizing intermediate conductivity, eg orbital electron occupancy, and adsorption free energy. Simultaneously, the reduced energy levels of Mn’s eg and t2g orbitals strengthen the interaction between Ni 3d/Mn 3d and Se 2p orbitals, promoting synergistic coupling, optimizing the electronic structure, and ensuring electrocatalytic stability. Consequently, the optimized catalyst exhibits remarkable performance, requiring overpotentials of 54.0 ± 0.5 mV and 235.0 ± 0.5 mV for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER), respectively, at 10 mA cm−2, while maintaining excellent stability. This work establishes a universal paradigm for d-band center manipulation in electrocatalyst design, providing critical insights for developing advanced energy conversion systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The urgent global energy dilemma and mounting environmental challenges have catalyzed substantial interest in developing eco-friendly and renewable energy solutions [1–3]. Hydrogen energy, with its remarkable energy density and environmentally friendly characteristics, has emerged as a promising solution to address growing energy needs while minimizing carbon emissions [4,5]. Electrochemical water splitting, comprising the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) at the cathode and anode respectively, is recognized as a clean and efficient approach for hydrogen generation [6–9]. Nevertheless, the inhibited reaction kinetics of OER remains a critical limitation in enhancing the overall efficiency of this process [10,11]. While noble metals such as Pt and Ru/Ir-based oxides demonstrate superior catalytic activity for both HER and OER, their large-scale implementation is constrained by limited availability and exorbitant costs [12]. Consequently, transition metal-based electrocatalysts, which are abundant and cost-effective, have garnered significant attention as viable alternatives [13]. Despite these advances, a major challenge remains: the compromise between catalytic performance and stability, where increased activity often leads to reduced durability [14]. Since both stability and efficiency are crucial for overall performance, there is an imperative demand for novel strategies to enhance durability without sacrificing catalytic activity, ideally by optimizing the intrinsic properties of these electrocatalysts.

The spin state configuration of transition metal centers has become a pivotal determinant in electrocatalytic systems, profoundly modulating catalytic structures, energy landscapes, and redox dynamics by governing d-orbital electron distribution and modifying electronic structures that dictate reaction pathways [15]. The spatially delocalized characteristics of d-orbital electron wavefunctions allow fine-tuned regulation of metal spin states through external stimuli such as ligand field engineering, magnetic fields, or lattice strain [16]. This tunability facilitates the utilization of spin–orbit coupling effects, maximizing electrocatalytic efficiency and stability at active sites [17]. High-spin configurations enhance d-orbital electron delocalization, redistribute electronic density, optimize reaction pathways, and minimize activation energy thresholds, enhancing reaction dynamics, particularly in multi-step redox processes [18]. For example, Yao et al. demonstrated that Ni-doping in MnO2 catalysts restructured electronic properties, achieving optimal d-electron spin configurations and increased eg orbital filling through controlled cation substitution [19]. Similarly, our group used fluorine doping to elevate eg and t2g orbital energy levels, fine-tuning oxygenated species adsorption and promoting catalyst surface reconstruction [20]. Low-spin configurations confer stability benefits by localizing electrons, reducing structural reorganization and lattice distortion at active sites [21]. This corrosion resilience arises from the electronic structure’s ability to withstand oxidative/reductive dissolution during cycling, along with regulated intermediate binding that prevents surface passivation, guaranteeing prolonged durability [22]. For instance, Fu et al. strategically incorporated Sn atoms with a 5s25p2 valence electron configuration into MnO2, where the σ molecular bonding orbitals between Sn 5 s and O 2p orbitals exhibit markedly lower energy, thus promoting the formation of more thermodynamically stable lattice structure [17]. Wang et al. synthesized NiFe-LDH with a high density of low-spin Fe3+ sites by doping with V ions and incorporating multiple vacancy defects, where the low-spin Fe3+ configuration (t2g 5eg 0) facilitates optimal interaction with oxide intermediates at the Fe sites during the OER process, thereby ensuring exceptional OER stability [23]. These findings suggest that dual-metal orbital engineering—elevating eg/t2g orbitals in one metal while lowering them in another—can synergistically enhance active site reactivity and bulk stability through tailored d-band center modulation and charge transfer kinetics.

Herein, this work proposes a dual-metal orbital engineering strategy to achieve synergistic optimization of active site reactivity and bulk-phase stability through precise d-band center modulation and improved charge transfer kinetics. Specifically, a multiphase flower-like Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs catalyst is constructed via controlled spin state regulation, comprising NiSe2-MnSe heterostructured nanosheets, sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs), and silver (Ag) nanoparticles. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal that the asymmetric structure between NiSe2 and MnSe significantly elevates the energy levels of Ni’s eg and t2g orbitals, optimizing intermediate conductivity, eg orbital electron occupancy, and adsorption free energy. Simultaneously, the reduction in Mn’s eg and t2g orbital energy levels enhances the synergistic coupling between Ni 3d/Mn 3d orbitals and Se 2p orbitals, effectively tuning the electronic structure of Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs to ensure electrocatalytic stability. The optimized Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs catalyst demonstrates exceptional catalytic performance in alkaline electrolyte and maintains remarkable durability with minimal activity decay during prolonged operation. This study not only advances the development of efficient bifunctional electrocatalysts for water splitting but also establishes a novel approach for precise d-band center engineering, offering valuable insights for designing advanced electrocatalytic systems.

Manganese sulfate monohydrate (MnSO4·H2O, ≥99.0 %), urea (H2NCONH2, ≥99.5 %), selenium powder (Se, ≥99.9 %), and silver nitrate (AgNO3, ≥99.8 %) were purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ammonium fluoride (NH4F, ≥96.0 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥98.0 %), nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, ≥98.0 %), thiourea (CH4N2S, ≥99.0 %), and ethylene glycol (HOCH2CH2OH, ≥98.0 %) were obtained from Tianjin Kaitong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Coffee grounds were powdered using a cell disrupter in three consecutive cycles. The powder was then stirred in 1.0 M H2SO4 for 12 h, followed by pH adjustment to 7 using a sodium hydroxide solution. After centrifugation, the powder was dried. Subsequently, 3.2 g of the treated coffee grounds and 1.6 g of thiourea were dispersed in 60 mL of deionized water. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, sealed, and heated at 180 °C for 12 h, yielding a dark brown solution. The crude product was purified through dialysis for 2 days, and the black S-CQDs were obtained after freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots modified NiMn layered double hydroxides supported on nickel foam
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF, 30.0 mg of S-CQDs, 431.9 mg of MnSO4·H2O, 522.5 mg of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 1717.7 mg of CO(NH2)2, and 529.7 mg of NH4F were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. The resulting suspension was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, and pretreated nickel foam (NF) was added. The autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 12 h.

Silver nanoparticles were synthesized by dissolving 1.7 g of (C6H9NO)n in 10 mL of ethylene glycol and heating the solution in an oil bath at 160 °C until it turned from colorless to light yellow. Subsequently, 10 mL of ethylene glycol containing 170.0 mg of AgNO3 was added dropwise to the solution. The reaction was maintained at this temperature for 4 h, resulting in a brown colloidal dispersion. After cooling to room temperature, acetone was added to induce precipitation. The precipitate was washed with acetone and collected to obtain small-sized silver nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of silver nanoparticles and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots modified NiMn layered double hydroxides supported on nickel foam
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of Ag/NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF, 50 mg of silver nanoparticles was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water and mixed with NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The mixture was heated at 120 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the Ag/NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF was washed and dried for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of silver nanoparticles and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots modified NiMn selenide supported on nickel foam
ce:section.ce:para: A Na2SeO4 solution was prepared by dissolving 1.1 g of selenium powder and 0.4 g of sodium hydroxide in 160 mL of deionized water. The solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h to obtain the Na2SeO4 solution. To synthesize Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF, Ag/NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF was immersed in 30 mL of the Na2SeO4 solution in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The resulting Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF was collected, washed, and dried for further use.

All electrochemical tests were conducted in 1.0 M KOH using a three-electrode system. The working electrode was the as-fabricated electrode (1 × 1 cm2), while a saturated calomel electrode and a carbon rod served as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. Before testing, the electrodes were stabilized through 20 cyclic voltammetry (CV) cycles at 50 mV s−1 within voltage ranges of 0.1–0.7 V (OER) and −1.3 to −0.7 V (HER). Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was performed at 5 mV s−1 to generate polarization curves, from which Tafel slopes were derived. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted at 1.1 V (OER) and −0.1 V (HER) vs. RHE, with a frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 105 Hz and a 5 mV amplitude. Electrode stability was tested by comparing LSV curves before and after 5000 CV cycles at 200 mV s−1 within 1.2–1.6 V (OER) and −0.3–0.1 V (HER) vs. RHE. Long-term stability was assessed via chronopotentiometry at a fixed current density for 100 h. The electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) was calculated from CV curves in non-faradaic regions (0–0.1 V for OER, −0.1–0 V for HER) at scan rates of 20–100 mV s−1. Turnover frequency (TOF) was estimated to evaluate OER activity under varying overpotentials (η).

Here, n is the number of active sites (mol) determined from CV measurements at 50 mV s−1 within 0.9–1.7 V (OER) and −0.2–0.6 V (HER) in 1.0 M PBS (pH ≈ 7). Q is the integral charge obtained by subtracting the blank value from the CV curve. ν is the stoichiometric number of electrons transferred (2 for HER, 4 for OER), j is the current density (mA cm−2) at a specific overpotential, F is the Faraday constant (≈96485C mol−1), and A is the electrode’s geometric area (cm2).

Microstructure characterization was performed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU8000), while X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku TTR-III) was employed to analyze the crystal phase structure. Surface elemental composition and chemical states were determined via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi) with monochromatic Al Kα radiation (hγ = 1486.6 eV). Microstructural properties and gas adsorption capacity were evaluated through N2 adsorption/desorption analysis (Quantachrome Instruments). Additionally, molecular structure and chemical composition were investigated using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and wettability was assessed by water contact angle measurements.

The comprehensive preparation process of the flower-like heterostructure Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. Nickel and manganese salts serve as precursor materials, while S-CQDs are incorporated to facilitate the growth of the bimetallic NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF on NF [24]. As shown in Fig. S1a, TEM images reveal nearly spherical S-CQDs with uniform size distribution. Fig. S1b presents XRD analysis, indicating S-CQDs are amorphous carbon materials with a broad peak at 2θ = 21.9° corresponding to the (002) crystal plane. Fig. S1c displays the FT-IR spectrum, where the broad band at 3400 cm−1 is attributed to –OH stretching, confirming hydroxyl group passivation. Peaks at 1590 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 correspond to CC and CO stretching, respectively, while the band at 1120 cm−1 indicates the presence of CS bonds, enhancing surface activity. Fig. S1d shows the fluorescence emission of S-CQDs under 365 nm UV light, confirming their excellent fluorescent properties. The inset illustrates the color change of the S-CQDs solution from brown to light blue under UV light, further verifying their fluorescence conversion characteristics. Subsequently, Ag nanoparticles with a nearly spherical morphology are deposited onto the surface of NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF via a hydrothermal method, forming Ag/NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF [25]. A selenization step is then performed to promote the formation of the asymmetric NiSe2/MnSe structure. This structure modifies the electronic configuration and d-band center, enhances intermediate adsorption, and accelerates phase transitions during HER and OER, leading to excellent electrocatalytic performance [26]. SEM is used to characterize the microstructures of NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF, Ag/NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF, and Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF. As shown in Fig. 1b and Fig. 1e, NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF exhibits a unique rose-like cross-linked nanosheet array structure [27]. These well-ordered nanosheets interweave to form an open framework that facilitates electrolyte ion penetration and effective contact. After hydrothermal treatment, dense Ag nanoparticles are uniformly distributed across the flower-like nanostructure (Fig. 1c and f), demonstrating the effective deposition of Ag on NiMn-LDH and promoting the exposure of numerous active sites [28]. After selenization, the flower-like structure of Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF is preserved (Fig. 1d and g), ensuring rapid ion transport [29]. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) reveals lattice spacings of 0.2650 nm for the (210) plane of NiSe2, 0.1728 nm for the (311) plane of MnSe, and 0.2390 nm for the (111) plane of Ag (Fig. 1h). These ordered lattice fringes confirm the successful formation of the NiSe2/MnSe heterojunction [30]. The close interface between NiSe2 and MnSe enhances interfacial charge transfer and improves ion diffusion during electrocatalytic reactions [31]. Ag nanoparticles, known for their high conductivity, enhance electron migration across the heterojunction interface, substantially boosting the catalytic reaction rate. Geometric strain analysis shows that Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF exhibits significant tensile or compressive strain along the lattice directions (Fig. 1i), enhancing interactions between reactants and the catalyst surface. Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns confirm the unique lattice features of Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF (Fig. 1j), corresponding to the (210) plane of NiSe2 and the (222) plane of MnSe, respectively.

The phase compositions of NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF, Ag/NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF, and Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF are analyzed and confirmed using XRD. As shown in Fig. 2 a, NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF and Ag/NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF exhibit similar diffraction peaks corresponding to NiMnO2(OH) (JCPDS No. 43–0318). This is attributed to the presence of anions in the interlayer of NiMn-LDH, which restricts the binding of positively charged Ag+ ions to the interlayer or their substitution of Ni2+ and Mn2+ ions, preventing their incorporation into the NiMn-LDH lattice. This suggests that the introduction of plasma metals does not significantly alter the crystallinity of the hydrotalcite structure [32]. Notably, no diffraction peaks corresponding to Ag are observed, indicating that the Ag nanoparticles are either small in size or present in low quantities. The XRD peaks of Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF at 29.9°, 33.6°, 36.9°, 50.7°, 55.5°, 57.8°, and 72.6° correspond to the (200), (210), (211), (023), (311), (321), and (421) lattice planes of NiSe2 (JCPDS No. 88–1711). Additionally, the peaks at 26.5°, 43.9°, and 52.1° are associated with the (111), (220), and (311) lattice planes of MnSe (JCPDS No. 73–1742). The coexistence of NiSe2 and MnSe phases demonstrates the successful conversion of NiMn-LDH into an asymmetric NiSe2/MnSe heterostructure [33]. The elemental composition and chemical environment of the samples are analyzed using XPS. The survey spectrum of Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF (Fig. S2) confirms the presence of Ni, Mn, Se, Ag, C, and S. In the high-resolution Ni 2p spectrum (Fig. 2b), two spin–orbit doublets are observed [34]. The peaks at 878.2 and 860.9 eV correspond to Ni3+ for Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2, respectively, while the peaks at 873.1 and 855.2 eV are attributed to Ni2+ for Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2. The broader peaks at 880.8 and 863.2 eV are assigned to satellite peaks [35,36]. Similarly, the high-resolution Mn 2p spectrum (Fig. 2c) shows two spin–orbit doublets. The peaks at 653.8 and 646.4 eV correspond to Mn4+ for Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2, respectively, while the peaks at 652.2 and 641.4 eV are associated with Mn3+ for Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2 [37,38]. Notably, the Ni 2p peaks of Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF exhibit a negative binding energy shift compared to Ag/NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF, while the Mn 2p peaks show a positive binding energy shift. This indicates charge transfer from Mn atoms to Ni atoms. The upward shift of Mn 2p and the downward shift of Ni 2p confirm charge redistribution and strong electronic interactions within the Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF heterostructure [39]. The Ag 3d spectrum is deconvoluted into two peaks (Fig. 2d), corresponding to Ag 3d3/2 at 373.9 eV and Ag 3d5/2 at 367.9 eV [40,41]. The Se 3d spectrum is also deconvoluted into two peaks (Fig. 2e), corresponding to Se 3d3/2 at 58.5 eV and Se 3d5/2 at 54.6 eV [42].

The surface functional groups of the synthesized samples are further investigated using FT-IR spectroscopy in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 (Fig. 2f). Peaks observed at approximately 446.8 cm−1, 1114.1 cm−1, 3411.5 cm−1, 854.7 cm−1, and 1444.8 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of Ni-O [43], CS [44], –OH [45], and CO3 2– [46], respectively. The FT-IR spectra of Ag/NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF and NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF are nearly identical. Notably, after selenization, the Mn-Se [47] and Ni-Se [48] stretching vibrations appear at 471.1 cm−1 and 599.7 cm−1, respectively, providing additional evidence for the successful synthesis of Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF. Additionally, the specific surface area and pore size distribution of the samples are evaluated through nitrogen adsorption–desorption measurements (Fig. 2g and Fig. 2h). All samples displayed type IV adsorption–desorption isotherms and H3 hysteresis loops, characteristic of mesoporous structures. The hysteresis at higher pressures (P/P0 ≥ 0.4) indicates the presence of mesopores [49]. The specific surface areas of NiMn-LDH/NF, NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF, Ag/NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF, and Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF are calculated to be 14.8 m2 g−1, 13.7 m2 g−1, 33.9 m2 g−1, and 32.2 m2 g−1, respectively. Additionally, the average pore sizes are 3.4 nm, 12.4 nm, 17.4 nm, and 24.2 nm, respectively, further confirming their mesoporous nature. The increased specific surface area and mesopore richness facilitate rapid reactant diffusion, reducing mass transfer resistance and enhancing the material’s conductivity and electrocatalytic performance [50]. The surface properties of the catalysts are assessed by measuring the contact angle of water droplets (Fig. 2i). Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF exhibits superior hydrophilicity with a contact angle of 43.5°, compared to NiMn-LDH/S-CQDs/NF (67.1°). This improves hydrophilicity enhances surface wettability and gas transport during water electrolysis, promoting efficient bubble release and water dissociation, thereby improving the material’s catalytic efficiency [51,52].

The electrocatalytic performance for HER is evaluated at room temperature in a 1.0 M KOH solution using a three-electrode system [53]. LSV results (Fig. 3 a and Fig. S3) show that Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF requires only a 54.0 ± 0.5 mV overpotential to achieve a current density of −10 mA cm−2, significantly lower than the 120.0 ± 0.5 mV for Ag/NiSe2/S-CQDs/NF, 173.0 ± 0.5 mV for Ag/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF, and 261.0 ± 0.5 mV for NF. This value is comparable to the 50.0 ± 0.5 mV of Pt/C, demonstrating that constructing a heterojunction generates a synergistic effect to enhance HER performance [54]. Similarly, Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF demonstrates efficient HER activity in seawater with a low overpotential of 73.0 ± 0.5 mV (Fig. S4a). Notably, the HER activity of Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF surpasses most recently reported nickel selenide-based electrocatalysts (Fig. S5). The Tafel slope, a key indicator of reaction kinetics, is presented in Fig. 3b. Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF exhibits a Tafel slope of 96.2 ± 0.5 mV dec−1, significantly lower than the 191.3 ± 0.5 mV dec−1 for Ag/NiSe2/S-CQDs/NF, 289.5 ± 0.5 mV dec−1 for Ag/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF, and 306.2 ± 0.5 mV dec−1 for NF, though higher than the 28.5 ± 0.5 mV dec−1 for Pt/C. A lower Tafel slope indicates faster reaction kinetics, attributed to increased active sites, reduced ion transfer distance, and accelerated ion transfer rate [55]. To further analyze charge transfer properties, impedance spectra are evaluated based on the equivalent circuit model derived from the Nyquist plot (Fig. 3c). Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF exhibits the lowest charge transfer resistance, attributed to the high conductivity and rapid charge transfer capabilities of the heterostructure [56]. The turnover frequency (TOF) of the samples is tested in a 1.0 M PBS solution to evaluate the intrinsic reaction rate (Fig. 3d). Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF exhibits the highest TOF value, further confirming its superior catalytic efficiency. Electrochemical surface area (ECSA) studies based on Cdl reveal that Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF displays the highest Cdl value of 5.0 ± 0.2 mF cm−2, outperforming Ag/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF (1.5 ± 0.2 mF cm−2) and Ag/NiSe2/S-CQDs/NF (2.6 ± 0.2 mF cm−2) (Fig. 3e and Fig. S6). This indicates that the heterointerface effectively increases the availability of active sites, enhancing HER activity [57]. Finally, the long-term stability of Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF during continuous HER is assessed (Fig. 4 f). At a current density of 10 mA cm−2, the potential remained stable over 96 h, demonstrating excellent durability.

The OER performance of the samples is evaluated in a 1.0 M KOH electrolyte using a three-electrode system. The Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF electrocatalyst demonstrates exceptional OER performance. The LSV curve (Fig. 4a) reveals that Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF requires only a 235.0 ± 0.5 mV overpotential to achieve a current density of 10 mA cm−2, significantly lower than Ag/NiSe2/S-CQDs/NF (241.0 ± 0.5 mV), Ag/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF (343.0 ± 0.5 mV), and NF (495.0 ± 0.5 mV). Moreover, Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF exhibits excellent OER performance of 243.0 ± 0.5 mV in seawater (Fig. S4b). Compared to recently reported nickel selenide-based OER catalysts (Fig. S7), this work exhibits superior performance. The reaction kinetics are further investigated using Tafel slopes (Fig. 4b). Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF shows a Tafel slope of 80.7 ± 0.5 mV dec−1, significantly lower than Ag/NiSe2/S-CQDs/NF (112.3 ± 0.5 mV dec−1), Ag/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF (160.3 ± 0.5 mV dec−1), and NF (212.2 ± 0.5 mV dec−1). The lower Tafel slope indicates faster charge transfer kinetics and enhanced OER reaction rates. The electrochemical performance further confirms the successful construction of the heterostructure, as shown in Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d. Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF exhibits the lowest charge transfer resistance and superior intrinsic electrocatalytic activity. The electrochemical surface area (ECSA), reflecting active site exposure, is also evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4e and Fig. S8, the Cdl values for Ag/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF, Ag/NiSe2/S-CQDs/NF, and Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF are 2.1 ± 0.2, 3.3 ± 0.2, and 4.5 ± 0.2 mF cm−2, respectively. This confirms that Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF possesses the highest density of catalytic active sites, validating the effectiveness of the phase engineering strategy. Finally, as demonstrated in Fig. 4f, Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF exhibits long-term stability, showing negligible performance decay after 96 h of testing at 10 mA cm−2, confirming its excellent durability and sustained catalytic activity.

To evaluate the structural stability of the Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF composite, the sample is subjected to 5000 electrochemical cycles and analyzed by SEM, XRD, and XPS (Fig. 5 ). SEM images confirm the preservation of the material’s uniform nanostructure without fracture, agglomeration, or degradation, indicating robust morphological stability. XRD analysis reveals no significant changes in the crystal structure, with diffraction peaks at 29.8°, 33.4°, 36.9°, 50.6°, 55.6°, 57.8°, and 72.7° assigned to NiSe2 (JCPDS No. 88-1711) and peaks at 26.6°, 44.0°, and 52.2° corresponding to MnSe (JCPDS No. 73-1742). The sharp and persistent peaks demonstrate excellent crystallinity and phase stability. XPS spectra show minor shifts in Se 3d and Ag 3d binding energies, likely due to partial Se-Mn/Ni bond breakage or slight Ag nanoparticle detachment, but no significant chemical changes or new species formation. These results affirm the material’s chemical and electrochemical durability under HER and OER conditions [58].

Based on the OER and HER performance, Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF serves as both anode and cathode in assembling the electrolyzer. As depicted in Fig. 6 a, the Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF||Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF demonstrates enhanced efficiency in alkaline water splitting, requiring only 1.5 ± 0.1 V compared to the 1.8 ± 0.1 V needed for NF||NF. This improvement is attributed to the material’s rich surface functional groups and excellent electrical conductivity, which facilitate the adsorption of water molecules and reaction intermediates. Additionally, these properties ensure a stable electron transfer pathway between the electrolyte and the catalyst surface, thereby optimizing the water splitting process [59,60]. Furthermore, after operating for 168 h at different current densities, no significant change is observed (Fig. 6b). To evaluate the Faradaic efficiency and electron utilization of Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF||Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF, hydrogen and oxygen generated during water splitting are collected using the water displacement method, as shown in Fig. 6c. The relationship between gas volume and time is illustrated in Fig. 6c1-8. After 70 min, the volume ratio of H2 to O2 approaches the theoretical value of 2:1 (Fig. 6d). These findings reveal that the Faradaic efficiency of the Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF||Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF system approaches 100 %, indicating its high electron utilization efficiency [61]. Compared to recently reported nickel selenide-based electrolyzes (Fig. 6e), this work demonstrates superior potential, further validating the effectiveness of constructing asymmetric heterointerfaces.

DFT calculations are systematically conducted to elucidate the electronic structure characteristics of the flower-like Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF heterostructure and their correlation with its exceptional catalytic performance [62]. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the charge density distribution analysis of NiSe2/MnSe heterostructure reveals significant electron redistribution at the heterointerface between adjacent NiSe2 and MnSe components [63]. This pronounced interfacial electron redistribution establishes an efficient electron transfer pathway, significantly enhancing HER and OER activities of the Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF catalyst [64]. The density of states (DOS) analysis, presented in Fig. 7b–c, demonstrates that both NiSe2 and MnSe, as well as their heterostructure, exhibit metallic characteristics with continuous DOS across the Fermi level [65]. Notably, the NiSe2/MnSe heterostructure shows a higher DOS value (−1.0 eV) compared to pristine NiSe2 (−1.3 eV), accompanied by enhanced d-band intensity of metal atoms near the Fermi level. These features indicate superior electron mobility in the heterostructure. Comparative analysis of DOS near the Fermi level (Fig. S9) reveals that NiSe2 in the heterojunction significantly contributes to the electronic structure modulation of MnSe, generating a higher concentration of active electrons for catalytic processes.

To gain deeper insights into the electronic structure modifications, crystal field theory calculations are performed to analyze the 3d orbital energy level variations of Ni and Mn centers in different configurations (Fig. 7d–e). The heterojunction construction induces significant changes in the orbital energy levels [66]. The Ni eg and t2g orbitals show a substantial energy elevation, enhancing the Ni atom’s participation in redox reactions by increasing its electron donation and acceptance capabilities. Simultaneously, the energy level reduction of the Mn eg and t2g orbitals leads to a shift in the electron density of Mn atoms, reducing electronic shielding effects. This energy disparity promotes direct electron interactions between Ni and Mn, strengthening the d-d orbital coupling and contributing to overall structural stabilization. This effect minimizes the risks of surface reconstruction and deactivation [67]. The asymmetric electronic modulation through orbital hybridization achieves an optimal balance between catalytic activity enhancement and structural stability. The catalytic performance is further evaluated through Gibbs free energy analysis of key reaction steps (Fig. 7f–g and Fig. S10). Regarding the OER performance, the reaction Gibbs free energy analysis of oxygen intermediate adsorption (*OOH, *OH, *O) reveals that NiSe2/MnSe exhibits a significantly reduced energy barrier (2.3 eV) compared to NiSe2 (2.5 eV) and MnSe (2.7 eV), respectively. For the HER, the asymmetric electronic structure enhances d-p orbital hybridization, resulting in a substantial reduction of ΔG*H for NiSe2/MnSe (1.3 eV) compared to pristine NiSe2 (1.8 eV) and MnSe (2.0 eV). This modification facilitates weaker hydrogen species binding and promotes efficient H+ release and transfer [67,68]. The heterointerface between NiSe2 and MnSe optimizes the electronic structure of Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF, precisely tuning the adsorption and desorption of oxygen intermediates [69]. These results demonstrate that the asymmetric electronic structure effectively lowers the reaction energy barriers, optimizing the catalytic activity of the Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF heterostructure.

In summary, a flower-like NiMn selenide nanoarchitecture, modified with silver nanoparticles and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs) has been successfully constructed on nickel foam (NF). Structural characterization has revealed its unique layered nanosheet open structure, featuring large surface area and multi-dimensional interfaces. These properties endow Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF with abundant active sites and enhanced gas release capability. Additionally, the heterostructure between the two metal selenides exhibits an asymmetric interface electronic structure, enabling the catalyst to demonstrate excellent electrocatalytic performance. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal that the hybridization of Ni 3d, Mn 3d, and Se 2p orbitals significantly elevates the energy levels of the eg and t2g orbitals of Ni, while markedly reducing those of Mn. This results in enhanced electrical conductivity, faster charge transfer kinetics, and superior catalytic activity and durability. Remarkably, the system delivers a current density of 10 mA cm−2 with remarkably low overpotentials of 54.0 ± 0.5 mV for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and 235.0 ± 0.5 mV for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), surpassing the performance of most recently reported Ni/Mn-based catalysts [70–72]. This study provides new insights into the relationship between asymmetric electronic structures and catalytic behavior in band engineering. The future work focuses on optimizing and scaling the Ag/NiSe2/MnSe/S-CQDs/NF catalyst, advancing its energy applications, and exploring asymmetric electronic structures to drive sustainable innovation in catalysis and materials.
